result.lens: Contrarian: the consensus is wrong. Position A sharpened: chi(R^2) = 5, and the clamp (the program's one missing object) is non-realizable because realizability and rainbow-forcing are mutually exclusive on Euclidean circle interfaces. Rider (dark horse): the 5-vs-6-vs-7 dispute is a definability split, so the repo's two live threads are measuring different invariants and can never meet.
result.first_principles:
Position A: chi(R^2) = 5. The consensus posterior (chi = 6 via a clamp from a new chi-5 UDG) mistakes lineage-wide freedom evidence for a construction gap when it is actually a law.

Where the Euclidean content lives. The three controls isolate exactly two facts about the round unit circle: (F1) a unit chord subtends exactly 60 degrees, so any section of a UDG by a unit circle is a subgraph of the 60-degree circulant, a disjoint union of paths and regular-hexagon C6s, hence bipartite; (F2) two distinct unit circles meet in at most 2 points. On R^1 circle sections are at most 2 isolated points (chi = 2, nothing to force). On Q^2 parity kills everything (chi = 2). On L^infty the unit sphere contains unit segments, sections contain edges everywhere and K4 exists, so chi = 4 = omega: clique-driven, no mystery. R^2 is the unique regime where circle sections are nonempty yet bipartite: every unit of chromatic power above omega = 3 must be imported globally onto a locally bipartite interface.

By de Bruijn-Erdos plus criticality, chi(R^2) >= 6 iff some 5-colorable finite UDG carries a rainbow-forced set (all 5 colors appear in every proper 5-coloring) on ONE unit circle; Theorem R relaxes this to TWO circles (the clamp). So the entire 5-vs-6 question is: can a 5-colorable UDG rainbow-force a one-or-two-circle set? Such a set has internal chi <= 4 (theorem: two bipartite circulant sections, Delta <= 4, Brooks plus omega <= 3), conjecturally <= 3. Rainbow-forcing it means importing forcing depth >= 2 across a bipartite interface; de Grey's jump to 5 imported depth 1 onto brittle 4-colorings. At 5 colors every probe ever run finds zero rigidity: 2.29M pairs free (L57), port relations confined to the primitive monoid (L55), and every rainbow-forced set the program ever produced (L27/L28, sizes 22-27) failed unit-cocircularity 0/97 and 0/92. Contrarian thesis: rainbow-forcing and realizability are mutually exclusive in the plane, and the ladder terminates at 5.

conjectures.name: CFC (Cocircular Freedom Conjecture)
conjectures.statement: For every finite unit-distance graph R in R^2 with chi(R) <= 5 and every one or two points p, q in R^2 (p = q allowed), set S = V(R) intersect (C(p,1) union C(q,1)). Then some proper 5-coloring of R uses at most 4 colors on S (S is never rainbow-forced). Two corollaries are part of the claim: (a) the single-circle case p = q is EQUIVALENT to chi(R^2) <= 5 (a 6-critical UDG minus a vertex would rainbow-force its link circle; conversely a non-vertex rainbow-forced circle yields a chi-6 UDG by adding one apex), so CFC + de Grey gives chi(R^2) = 5 exactly; (b) the two-circle case p != q implies, via the repo's Theorem R, that NO flexible color-clamp is unit-distance realizable. The 4-color analog of CFC is FALSE (de Grey's graph minus a vertex rainbow-forces its link in 4-colorings); CFC asserts the forcing ladder terminates exactly at k = 5.
conjectures.falsifiable_prediction: The rich-center rainbow scan (experiment below) returns SAT on every candidate locus of all 12 lineage graphs: for every point p with >= 6 vertices at exact unit distance, and every pair of >= 3-rich centers (p,q) with |p-q| not in {0,1}, a proper 5-coloring exists using <= 4 colors on S. A single UNSAT (with the extendability check SAT) refutes CFC and hands the program its missing object; UNSAT on a single circle directly yields a 6-chromatic UDG with |V|+1 vertices. CFC also predicts that no finite-UDG SAT certificate of chi >= 6 will ever exist.
conjectures.why_plausible: Four independent strands: (i) L57: all ~2.29M non-adjacent pairs in the lineage are free; (ii) L55: color symmetry confines single-port relations to the primitive monoid {0, I, J-I, J}, so forcing cannot be carried by narrow interfaces; (iii) across 57 learnings, rainbow-forcing and realizability have never coexisted: the only rainbow-forced sets ever produced (L27/L28 abstract bridge graphs, set sizes 22-27) failed unit-cocircularity 0/97 and 0/92; (iv) two-circle sets have internal chi <= 4 (and conjecturally 3), so rainbow-forcing them needs import depth >= 2, while the only proven Euclidean import mechanism (spindling off triangles) has depth 1.
conjectures.how_it_could_be_wrong: Lineage bias: all 12 chi-5 UDGs come from one hex-spindle recipe, and a new-principle chi-5 UDG could rainbow-force where the lineage cannot (this is precisely the consensus position). The universal quantifier over all UDGs rests on finite evidence. If chi(R^2) >= 6 for any reason, CFC is false by criticality. And the depth heuristic is not a theorem: L28's graded rainbow sets prove large-set forcing exists abstractly, so only realizability blocks it, which is exactly what is unproven.
conjectures.novelty_claim: The uniform one-or-two-circle freedom law, with the observation that its single-circle case is equivalent to chi(R^2) = 5 while its two-circle case kills Theorem R clamps, appears nowhere in the literature. Nearest prior art: de Grey's omega <= 3 conjecture (predicts 5 from clique structure, but the clamp escapes its premise; CFC is the strengthening that closes that escape), the repo's Lemma L (L42, which covers only |S| = 5, where rainbow implies pairwise forcing), and L28's graded rainbow forcing (abstract side only, never realized).

conjectures.name: TC (Two-Circle Three-Colorability)
conjectures.statement: Let p != q be points of R^2 and let S be any finite set contained in C(p,1) union C(q,1) with p, q not in S. Then the unit-distance graph induced on S has chi <= 3. (Provable now: chi <= 4, since by F1/F2 every vertex of S has degree <= 4, K5 is impossible because omega <= 3, and Brooks applies; a single circle gives a bipartite circulant. The conjecture is that 4 is never attained.)
conjectures.falsifiable_prediction: A counterexample is a realizable 4-chromatic two-circle configuration. Concrete search: enumerate combinatorial types (cyclic sequences of within-circle 60-degree edges and cross-circle unit chords) for n <= 12 points; each type is a small polynomial system in the center distance d and angles, decidable by sympy Groebner (well under the ~14-vertex feasibility ceiling); SAT-check 3-colorability of every realizable type. TC predicts all realizable types up to n = 12 are 3-colorable. Calibration check included: the Moser spindle (the canonical 4-chromatic Delta-4 UDG) is NOT two-circle-supported (its hub-plus-tips triple has circumradius 3/sqrt(11) != 1), and the odd wheel W5 needs a degree-5 hub, exceeding the Delta <= 4 cap.
conjectures.why_plausible: Within-circle components are paths and C6s (bipartite); cross edges form at most a 2-regular ladder per vertex; odd cycles exist (so chi = 3 is attained and the conjecture is tight) but every 4-critical Delta <= 4 graph I can name fails the two-circle support test. The cap Delta <= 4 comes from strict convexity plus roundness (each unit circle around a point of S meets the two carrier circles in at most 4 points), which is exactly the Euclidean rigidity the L-infinity detector demands.
conjectures.how_it_could_be_wrong: Algebraic coincidences could realize a 4-critical Delta <= 4 graph on two circles, e.g. d = sqrt(3) makes the two circle-intersection points exactly unit-separated, enabling spindle-like relatives. Brooks leaves genuine room and nothing structural forbids chi = 4. Note the failure mode is productive for the consensus: a 4-chromatic two-circle set would be the first locally-chi-4 interface, shrinking the clamp's forcing deficit from >= 2 to 1.
conjectures.novelty_claim: The chromatic number of unit-distance graphs supported on two unit circles has, to my knowledge, never been studied; single-circle bipartiteness is folklore. Nearest prior art: Chilakamarri's normed-plane UDG papers and circulant-graph coloring theory. The role of TC as a quantitative interface-deficit bound feeding a clamp impossibility argument is new.

conjectures.name: KM5 (No Frozen 5-Colorings of UDGs)
conjectures.statement: No finite planar unit-distance graph admits a Kempe-frozen proper 5-coloring, i.e. a 5-coloring with all five classes nonempty in which every pair of color classes induces a connected subgraph. (Stronger mechanism form: all proper 5-colorings of any finite UDG form a single Kempe equivalence class.) Frozen colorings are the canonical obstruction to Kempe mixing; KM5 is the proposed mechanism behind CFC: 5-coloring spaces of UDGs are everywhere Kempe-mobile, and mobility across bipartite circle interfaces is what dissolves would-be rainbow forcing. KM5 supports CFC heuristically; it does not imply it.
conjectures.falsifiable_prediction: Frozenness of a given coloring is an O(m) check (all 10 class-pair unions connected). Necessary edge count: the 10 class-pair edge sets partition E, each must connect its union, so m >= 4n - 10; lineage graphs (m/n about 4.5-5) CLEAR this threshold, so the conjecture is not vacuous on them and is genuinely at risk. Test: sample 10^4 SAT-generated 5-colorings per lineage graph plus simulated-annealing hill-climbs that maximize the number of connected class-pair unions with SAT repair; exhaustively enumerate all 5-colorings of UDGs up to ~12 vertices. Any frozen 5-coloring of any UDG refutes KM5.
conjectures.why_plausible: Hex-lattice-like patches have m about 3n < 4n - 10, so large lattice regions can never freeze; bipartite 60-degree circulant links starve local class-pair connectivity; no frozen coloring has ever surfaced in the program's tens of thousands of SAT witnesses (though nobody looked, which is the point).
conjectures.how_it_could_be_wrong: The edge-count check shows the dense chi-5 graphs are numerically capable of freezing; frozen k-colorings exist for abstract graphs below their max degree, and 5 is far below the lineage max degrees (up to 36), so there is room. A frozen 5-coloring may simply never have been hunted for.
conjectures.novelty_claim: Kempe equivalence has a literature (Las Vergnas-Meyniel, Mohar's conjecture, Bonamy-Bousquet-Feghali-Johnson on regular graphs), but Kempe properties of unit-distance graphs at k = 5, the m >= 4n - 10 freezing threshold for them, and the link to clamp non-realizability are all unstated anywhere I know.

conjectures.name: Definability Split at 5/6/7 (Soifer split, pinned)
conjectures.statement: chi(R^2) = 5 in ZFC, every proper 5-coloring of the plane is non-Lebesgue-measurable, and the measurable and Borel chromatic numbers are both 7 (Isbell tight in the definable world). Consequently the value 6 is attained by NO natural invariant, and the program's two live threads provably cannot meet: the finite-UDG thread tops out at 5 (by CFC) and the measurable SDP thread climbs past 6 to 7. The community's chi = 6 consensus is an artifact of averaging two invariants that differ.
conjectures.falsifiable_prediction: Three checks in this repo's stack: (i) the rich-center rainbow scan and all successor finite scans stay SAT forever (any finite chi >= 6 certificate kills the split); (ii) when the order-2 X_23 Lasserre solver lands, it certifies chi_m >= 6 (consistent), and larger configurations push toward 7 rather than plateauing at 6; (iii) sharp near-term probe: optimal dual witnesses of the SDP (O(2)-averaged J_0 Bochner kernel measures) should have NO finitely-supported extreme points, since a finitely-supported optimal dual would transfer the bound to a finite UDG and contradict (i); the order-1 L43 margin-0 solution's support structure is inspectable in cvxpy today.
conjectures.why_plausible: It is the unique position consistent with the program's full 57-learning experience: every finite probe ever run finds freedom (L45/L51/L56/L57), every measurable probe finds obstruction (Falconer, the repo's chi_m >= 6 thread). Falconer already proved the 4/5 prototype of the split's lower half. Voronov's theorem that map-type colorings need 7 is exactly what the upper half predicts for tame colorings. Shelah-Soifer shows AC-sensitivity of chromatic numbers of plane-like graphs is real.
conjectures.how_it_could_be_wrong: One finite UNSAT anywhere kills it; a constructed measurable 6-coloring (or any measurable 5-coloring) kills it; and the chi_B = 7 upper half currently has no valid proof sketch, since the known Borel/Steinhaus lower-bound blades are norm-blind and fail the L-infinity detector (L54 flagged exactly this), leaving only the rotation-sensitive J_0 route, which is unproven at 7.
conjectures.novelty_claim: Soifer conjectured AC-dependence in general and Falconer pinned chi_m >= 5, but the fully pinned split (5 in ZFC, 7 definably, 6 attained by nothing, with the corollary that the two research programs are measuring different invariants and cannot converge) has not been stated. Nearest prior art: Shelah-Soifer 2003, Falconer 1981, Voronov's map-coloring theorem, and the repo's own L54 col5/borel dark-horse worlds, of which this is the fusion.

experiment.description: RICH-CENTER RAINBOW SCAN: test Theorem R's object in its native geometric form on the realized lineage, which no logged experiment has done (L45/L56/L57 scanned only pairs of EXISTING vertices; the missing object's endpoints s,t are NEW vertices at circle centers). For each lineage graph R (start S199, then P_510): (1) enumerate candidate centers exactly: for every vertex pair u,v with 0 < |uv| < 2, compute the two intersection points of C(u,1) and C(v,1) in sympy over Q(sqrt3, sqrt11) (mpmath 50-digit prefilter, exact confirmation); dedupe; keep centers p with incidence |V intersect C(p,1)| >= 3, plus all vertex positions. (2) Single-circle pass: for each rich center p with incidence >= 6, one SAT query (cadical via python-sat): does a proper 5-coloring exist with color 5 unused on A = V intersect C(p,1)? By color symmetry one query decides rainbow-forcing. UNSAT means R plus one apex vertex is a 6-chromatic UDG: program over. This pass exploits a documented gap: Lemma L's closure (rainbow implies pairwise forced-different implies unit-K_k) is only valid at |S| = 5; for |S| >= 6 the first implication fails, as L28's own graded-rainbow sets of size 22-27 demonstrate, so this is NOT a re-run of the killed single-hub route. (3) Pair pass: pre-filter all center pairs (p,q) with |p-q| not in {0,1} and |S| >= 6 against ~300 cached SAT-sampled diverse 5-colorings (vectorized: discard any pair whose S already misses a color in some sample); exact SAT only on survivors; for any UNSAT, run the extendability query (forbid color 4 on A and color 5 on B): SAT there certifies a realizable clamp with forced-different virtual pair (s,t) at distance d = |p-q|; reproduce any UNSAT with a second solver (glucose) per repo discipline. (4) Cache witnesses under experiments/combinatorial/_cache/richcenter_rainbow/. DECISIVE BOTH WAYS: any hit = the program's missing object (or a chi-6 UDG outright), refuting CFC and embarrassing the contrarian; all-SAT = the lineage is dead in the FULL Theorem R sense (not just the vertex-pair sense), the first systematic evidence for CFC beyond L57, and a material posterior shift from chi = 6 toward chi = 5. Adversarial side-targets for the same session: hunt frozen 5-colorings (KM5 check, O(m) per sampled coloring) on the same graphs for free.
experiment.repo_feasibility: Fully within the existing stack: reuses shotC_forced_same.py's 12-graph loader, python-sat (cadical + glucose) for incremental queries with assumptions, sympy exact arithmetic in the lineage number fields, mpmath for prefiltering; no C compiler, no nauty needed. About 200 lines of new code in experiments/combinatorial/.
experiment.est_cost: Center enumeration: O(n^2) circle intersections, ~20k pairs for S199, minutes; rich-center incidence via sorted exact keys. Sampling filter kills nearly all center pairs cheaply; expected < 10^3 exact SAT decisions at 0.1-1 s each. Total: 1-2 h for S199, half a day for P_510, a weekend of compute for the full 12-graph sweep.

detector_self_check.Q2: All four conjectures are flexibility-shaped (upper-bound-flavored) claims, so the over-proving risk on Q^2 would be a lower bound leaking there; none is asserted: the only lower-bound input used anywhere is de Grey's chi >= 5, imported as a literature fact, never re-derived combinatorially. The freedom mechanism (bipartite 60-degree circulant links, two circles meeting in <= 2 points) is consistent-but-vacuous on Q^2, where chi = 2 makes every set trivially non-rainbow-forced for 5 colors. The experiment cannot even be stated nontrivially over Q^2: the rich centers live at sqrt(11)/sqrt(3) coordinates that mostly do not exist in Q^2, which is exactly where the Euclidean content should sit.
detector_self_check.Linf: The load-bearing facts fail in L^infty BY DESIGN: unit spheres there contain unit segments, sphere sections contain K4, so the bipartite-link law and TC's Delta <= 4 cap are Euclid-only. The L^infty analog of CFC is false (chi = 4 = omega there, and rainbow-forcing-at-4 on sphere sections is achieved by cliques), and the position neither needs nor claims it: nothing here proves >= 5 from normed-plane-generic structure, and the chi <= 5 direction of CFC rests on the 60-degree circulant and the <= 2-point circle intersection, neither of which holds for the square sphere. Conjecture 4's measurable upper half explicitly rejects the norm-blind Steinhaus blades L54 flagged and rides only on the rotation-sensitive J_0 kernel.
detector_self_check.R1: Conjectures 1-3 are pure combinatorics plus SAT, not measure theory, so the R^1 detector's target (rotation-blind measure arguments) does not arise; on R^1 a 'unit circle' is 2 points, so |S| <= 4 < 5 and CFC is trivially true while implying nothing about chi(R) = 2. Conjecture 4 is the only measure-flavored claim and its engine is the O(2)-averaged J_0 Bochner kernel, which is built from the planar rotation group and is silent on the line.
